text: The surgeon uses an atraumatic grasper to hold a small band of adhesion between the left lobe of the liver and the phobic string. The harmonic scalpel is used to gently cut the adhesion, freeing the liver from the ring. The lock of the ring is visible on the right side, appearing blue, while the rest of the ring is white.

The surgeon attempts to insert a gastric calibration tube but is unable due to the presence of the fauvist ring. The omentum of the lesser curvature is freed approximately twelve centimeters below the ring using a harmonic device, creating a retro gastric tunnel. A window is formed in the omentum, allowing for the use of an endo-GI linear stapler to divide the stomach and create a gastric pouch. The sleeve stomach is visible, and the surgeon plans to perform a side-to-side anastomosis using the stapler.

The surgeon creates a retro-gastric tunnel by lifting the stomach with the left hand, ensuring it is positioned above the pancreas to avoid injury. The harmonic device is used to carefully separate the stomach, with the silicon jaw kept close to prevent accidental perforation. The tunnel formation is accompanied by minor bleeding, which is coagulated. A window appears, allowing for the introduction of the endo-GI stapler. The grasper is visible over the anterior surface of the left lobe of the liver, indicating the tunnel's completion.

The surgeon uses an Endo GIA linear stapler with a blue cartridge to divide the dilated portion of the stomach, creating a gastric pouch. Despite the sleeve gastrectomy, additional cartridges are needed to complete the division. The gastric pouch is visibly long, designed to minimize complications given the presence of the phobias ring. The surgeon prepares to bring the jejunal loop for anastomosis.

The surgeon identifies the secondary gastric pedicle and lifts the omentum towards the transverse colon. The transverse colon is visible, and the omentum is further lifted to locate the start of the jejunum. The small intestine is pulled down to reveal anatomical landmarks, including the inferior mesenteric vein and ligamentum teres. The surgeon measures 250 centimeters of the jejunum for the anastomosis, ensuring no torsion occurs. An 8mm opening is made in both the gastric pouch and small intestine to prepare for the gastrojejunostomy.

The surgeon perforates the stomach and intestine, ensuring the mucosa is visible, and enlarges the incision using the vibrating jaw of the harmonic. The lumen of both the intestine and stomach is clearly visible. The endo-GI linear stapler is prepared for side-to-side anastomosis, with the face jaw positioned in the intestine and the cartilage jaw in the stomach. The assistant helps align the jaws parallel for controlled firing, completing the mini gastric bypass.

The surgeon completes the side-to-side anastomosis between the jejunum and the stomach, ensuring the internal suture line is intact with no visible bleeding. The mucosa of both the jejunum and stomach is clearly visible. Suturing is performed using a bi-directional barb suture with a pre-tied loop, applying full thickness, single-layer closure from out to in and in to out. The starter knot is secured without tying, using the open jaw of a grasper to tighten the loop.

The surgeon performs full-thickness suturing of the gastric pouch, ensuring airtight closure by including the mucosa. A leak test is conducted using 150 ml of methylene blue dye, with the jejunum and intestine clamped using atraumatic graspers. The closure progresses layer by layer, using a two-third curve needle with barb suture, while avoiding excessive traction on the needle. The lesser omentum is visible attached to the gastric loop during closure.

The surgeon uses a barb suture with laser cuts every millimeter to secure the tissue during the final stage of the mini gastric bypass closure. The suture holds the tissue effectively without slipping back, and a termination knot is added for double security.

The surgeon uses two needle holders to tighten the barb suture by pulling it with the open jaw, reinforcing the closure with additional bites to prevent leakage. The suture is passed through the loop and locked, ensuring a secure termination.

The surgeon tightens the barb suture by pulling with a needle holder and pressing the tissue with an open jaw, securing it with a square knot. The suture is terminated on healthy tissue using a Sarjan knot, and the needle is removed. An atraumatic clamp holds the afferent and efferent loops of the intestine as the anesthetist injects 150 mL of methylene blue dye to test for leaks at the anastomosis and suturing site. The dye distends the area, confirming no leaks.

The surgeon checks for leaks and bleeding after completing the mini gastric bypass procedure. The tissue collapses upon releasing the grasper, and the phobias ring is visible, indicating the completion of the surgery.
